"But he who heard and did nothing is like a man who built a house on the earth without a foundation, against which the stream beat vehemently; and immediately it fell. And the ruin of that house was great."
Points on Luke 6:44-49: Faith is the foundation. Faith produces action. Action apart from faith is vain. Obedience follows faith.
Please also consider reviewing the statement of faith regarding salvation.

Grace: It is an attribute of God by which He seeks the good of His creation (1 Pet 2:3). By His nature, God is gracious and bestows unmerited favour on mankind. God has prepared salvation and offered it to humans as a gift on the basis of His grace (Eph 2:8).

Repentance: metanoia means a change of mind. Repentance is agreeing with God about the problem, I am a sinner (Eph 2:1-3), my righteousness is like filthy rags before God (Isa 64:6), I cannot save myself (Rom 3:23), and also agreeing with God about the solution, Jesus is my Saviour. Repentance is necessary for salvation. It was preached by Jesus (Mat 4:17), and by Paul (Acts 17:30, 20:21). Although sorrow might accompany repentance, penitence is not repentance. Repentance cannot be a work someone performs to attain salvation. It is a change of mind to think the way God thinks.

Faith: It is the reasonable response of the heart to Christ by believing the evidence God has given. Faith is believing God and taking Him at His word. It involves transferring of trust to the Lord after hearing the Word of truth (Rom 10:17). Faith is a non-meritorious work. While it is something that the sinner does or more specifically responds with, it is not a work that needs to be done in the general sense of the word.

Faith and repentance work hand-in-hand in the process of salvation: The Spirit of God convicts a sinner of his sin (Jn 16:8-10). Under such conviction, the sinner can either repent or reject. The Word of God illuminates (Jn 1:9, Psa 119:130) the mind of the sinner (Jn 3:16, Acts 16:30-31). Under such illumination, the sinner can either respond in faith or in unbelief. The sinner who responds with obedience, repenting and responding in faith, is saved (Mk 1:15, Eph 1:13, Jn 5:24).

Discussion: Read each word defined above and discuss the main ideas that stand out to you. 1. Describe faith in your own words. 2. What is one example of someone who changes the direction they are going because they had a change in mind? 3. Describe this change of mind, known as repentance, in your own words. 4. If you had faith in God, realized you were on a path going away from Him, and felt a conviction to turn around, what would you do next?
The question of sign gifts is a peripheral theology, not a Gospel issue. Those who practice these gifts in their church would still be considered brothers and sisters in Christ.

Reasons for cessation: (1) Completion of the scriptural Canon, does not stand alone but combined with maturity of the Church provides a solid case for cessation (1 Cor 13:8). (2) Continuation of sign gifts leaves the door dangerously open for extra-biblical knowledge. (3) There is no need for multi-language gifts for proclaiming the Gospel anymore except in rare exceptional cases. (4) If the gifts were genuinely operative they would be more consistently found across denominations and mission fields. (5) If tongues were consistent with the early-church gift, organizations like New Tribes / Wycliffe would not still need to exist. (6) There are no genuine documented cases of valid practice of the gift with an interpreter. (7) There is no evidence in Scripture of privately practicing tongues as a prayer language. (8) 'Prayer tongue' is a coined term based on verses saying 'pray in a tongue or sing in a tongue' and 1 Cor 13 which refers to the tongues of men and angels. (9) Tongues is not encouraged and Paul's tone is hyperbole. (10) Everyone agrees the sign gifts will cease, the question is 'when'. (11) The 'need' for tongues was pertaining to dispersion of the 'Hebrew' Gospel to the 'World'. (12) Miracles continue but practice as 'gifts' is what has ceased. (13) 'Healers' have ceased. (14) Jesus says in Matt 12:39-41 that signs will not be given.
A consistent cessationist position would not require 'tongues' as proof of salvation.
Find examples of churches that are practicing the sign gifts well, there are none.

Reasons for continuation: (1) The interpretation of the biblical passages is not explicit on cessation. (2) 1 Cor 1:7 suggests a context of waiting for Christ's return as opposed to the cessationist reference to the 'Bible'. (3) Though they should never be the end goal for validation, the scope for signs still exists (1 Cor 14:24-25) and gifts were a part of the confirmation of Christ among them (1 Cor 1:6-8). (4) Early-church practice included the practice of prophetic words that were supposed to be judged by all (except women), and even Philip's daughters were known as prophets. (5) If the point was connected to Canon of scripture, it does not seem like Philip's daughters' prophesies or other prophesies by Agabus were documented or even included in scripture. (6) Paul distinguishes these gifts and prophesies from 'scripture' in 1 Cor 14:37 in which he clarifies that the prophets among them must recognize that Paul's words are the commands of God.

Scripture at face value indicates prayer in tongues without interpretation to be a personal matter. Tongues was never meant to be spoken as exhortation or instruction to someone else; it was always directed to God as praise or prayer, and those around heard what the individual was saying to God and were encouraged by it (see Acts 2:11, 1 Cor 14:14-18, 1 Cor 14:28). Tongues without interpretation as a prayer tongue through the Spirit is indicated by the following: 1 Cor 14:2; 1 Cor 14:4; 1 Cor 14:14-15; 1 Cor 14:18; 1 Cor 14:28.
Other references to consider on the question of sign gifts: 1 Thess 5:18-21; Acts 21; 1 Cor 12-14; 1 Cor 1:7.

Bare-text justifications for continuation: 'The Bible says so'; 1 Cor 12:31 ('earnestly desire the higher gifts'). Interesting observation to consider for those who say there is not enough scripture to support the sign gifts: count explicit references to the gifts compared with explicit references for cessation. Don't throw the baby out with the bath water on both extremes.

Questions for a cessationist: (1) Since it refers to the gifts being given by the Spirit as He wills, how can you confidently say someone who receives the gift did not receive it through the Holy Spirit if it is in line with the teaching of scripture and is motivated by love for the edification of the church? (2) If the Canon-of-scripture argument doesn't stand, what other logic / theology / verses can be used for cessationism? (3) When Paul is speaking in 'hyperbole' in other parts, he seems to make it very clear, why would he be so subtle here? (4) Are there scriptural examples of every other gift of the Spirit being practiced in the New Testament? (5) What motivates your disagreement with continuationism? (6) What is the role of the Holy Spirit in scripture and what is His role today?

Personal point of view (a bridging position): There is a fundamental difference in perspective between cessationists and continuationists, but encouragement can come from both points of view; the dream is to find a way to bridge the divide since scripture does not explicitly describe cessation. Paul's points about unity and love can be applied between denominational divides, and holding an openness to the gifts helps reduce the divides, believers could be much more edifying to one another and more effective together, but churches and theologies create natural invisible walls. The expectation of God using the gifts is higher in this view than in a cessationist's, since a cessationist might even say God will not act that way. These interpretive issues create the kind of situation that the Pharisees found themselves in, Jesus was very clearly defying their expectations and they hated Him and completely missed what God was doing. There is also a personal passion for 'faith' and the 'power' of faith for a believer who has both the Word of God and the Spirit of God; the sign gifts are often aligned to the fueling of faith or the presence of faith. At face value, the essential point of the passage is an active and ongoing desire to surrender and submit to the Holy Spirit for the sake of the church being built up. If there is agreement that some sign gifts were intended to last until the present day, then only a 'deductive theological' conclusion would produce cessation. While deductive theological conclusions have value, in this context they may primarily reflect a 'reaction' to extreme charismatic movements (which is a trademark of assembly Theology).

Passages this bridging view helps interpret and apply: John 7:38-39; 1 Jn 2:27; 1 Jn 3:24; 2 Cor 12:1; 2 Cor 12:7; Gal 3:2; Gal 3:3; Gal 3:5; Gal 5:16; Gal 5:17; Gal 5:18; Gal 6:8; Eph 1:13; Eph 1:17; Eph 2:22; Eph 3:16-21; Eph 4:30; Eph 5:18; Eph 6:10-18; Luke 3:16; Mark 1:8; Rom 8:1,14.
External theologians whose views on this subject are appreciated: Wayne Grudem, D.A. Carson, John Piper, Mike Winger, Matt Chandler. Three referenced YouTube videos are linked in the source.

Possible evidences cited for continuation, with caveats noted in the source: personal experience that is aligned with the interpretation of Scripture; testimony of salvation because of a 'word of knowledge' (objection/question raised: does that mean it supports the continued practice of such gifts?); testimonies of healing (objection raised: these occurrences do not prove continuationism, these cases are not valid points for the context of practice of the church).
Feedback from the group: desiring gifts was for the church as a unit to pray that people with those gifts would be added to the church, since gifts are given at the point of salvation.
We believe in One God (Deut 6:4, Mark 12:29). Monotheism is contrasted with other theories such as polytheism.
God created all things (Isa 45:7, Gen 1:1). This creator-claim is contrasted with evolution as an account of origins.
God sustains all things (Isa 45:7). This is contrasted with Deism, which holds that God created but does not actively uphold creation.
God has eternally existed (Hebrews 9:14, Deut 33:27, Psalm 90:2).

God exists eternally in three Persons (Mat 28:19, Matt 3:16, 2 Corinthians 13:14, John 15:26, John 16:3): The Father, The Son (Matthew 28:18), and The Holy Spirit (Acts 5:3-4). The three Persons are equal (Phil 2:6, John 14:6).

Divine attributes, definition: 'Attributes are the perfections which are predicated of the divine being in the scriptures or are visibly exercised by Him in His work of creation, redemption and providence.' (Louis Berkhof)

Divine attributes are classified as incommunicable and communicable. Incommunicable perfections are characteristics unique to God (e.g., self-existence, simplicity, immensity). Communicable perfections are characteristics transferable in part to humans (e.g., goodness, righteousness, love).

The three Persons are holy in all divine attributes, including: omniscient, holy, just, love, true, free, omnipotent, eternal, infinite, immutable (God is unchanging and unchangeable), omnipresent, and sovereign (1 Peter 1:2).
The Persons of the Godhead (1 Peter 1:2) perform distinct and harmonious roles in creation, providence, and redemption.
Creation and preservation are accomplished through the Son (John 1:3, 10; Col 1:16-17; 1 Cor 8:6; Heb 1:2-3, 10) and through the Spirit (Gen 1:2; Job 26:13; 32:8; 33:4; 34:14-15; Ps 104:30).
Redemption is accomplished through the Father (1 Chron 17:21; Isa 63:16; Gal 4:4-5) and through the Spirit (Heb 9:14; Rom 8:11).
Sanctification is accomplished through the Father (John 17:17; 1 Thess 5:23) and through the Son (1 Cor 1:30; Eph 5:25-27).

Summary statement: We believe in One God who created and sustains all things. God has eternally existed in three Persons; The Father, The Son, and The Holy Spirit. They are equal and holy in all divine attributes. The Persons of the Godhead perform distinct and harmonious roles in creation, providence, and redemption.

We believe that God the Father is the one God, the Father from whom are all things and for whom we live (1 Cor 8:6); the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms in Christ (Eph 1:3); one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6).
God the Father is eternal, He is over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6).

God the Father is the all-powerful Creator. He has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms in Christ (Eph 1:3); He is one God and Father of all, over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6); He has created all things, the mystery hidden for ages in God who has created all things (Eph 3:9); for from Him and through Him and to Him are all things, to Him be glory forever! Amen (Rom 11:36).

God the Father is the sustainer of the universe, from whom are all things and for whom we live (1 Cor 8:6); the One who accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will (Eph 1:11); who is over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6); the Father is the gardener (John 15:1).

God the Father has ordered all things according to His perfect will. He predestined believers to adoption as His legal heirs through Jesus Christ, according to the pleasure of His will (Eph 1:5); He has predestined according to the purpose of Him who accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will (Eph 1:11); His secret plan, the mystery hidden for ages in God who has created all things, is now being made known (Eph 3:9).

God the Father has done all this for His glory, for there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we live (1 Cor 8:6); for from Him and through Him and to Him are all things, to Him be glory forever! Amen (Rom 11:36).
God the Father has sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into this world to redeem sinful man.

In addition to His role as Father within the Godhead, God the Father adopts as His children all who come to Him in Christ; therefore all believers have the right to address Him as Abba Father. To all who have received Him, those who believe in His name, He has given the right to become God's children (John 1:12). All who are led by the Spirit of God are the sons of God (Rom 8:14). Believers did not receive the spirit of slavery leading again to fear, but received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, 'Abba, Father' (Rom 8:15). 'I will be a father to you, and you will be my sons and daughters,' says the All-Powerful Lord (2 Cor 6:18). Christ came to redeem those who were under the law, so that we may be adopted as sons with full rights (Gal 4:5). He chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world that we should be holy and blameless before Him in love (Eph 1:4). He did this by predestining us to adoption as His legal heirs through Jesus Christ, according to the pleasure of His will (Eph 1:5). In Christ we too have been claimed as God's own possession, since we were predestined according to the purpose of Him who accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will (Eph 1:11).

Verses cited in support of this article: John 1:12; Romans 8:14-15; Romans 11:36; 1 Corinthians 8:6; 2 Corinthians 6:18; Galatians 4:5; Ephesians 1:5-6, 11; Ephesians 4:6; Ephesians 3:9; John 15:1, 8.

Proposed addition (marked 'SHOULD WE ADD' in source, flagged for editorial decision): Adding to the article that God the Father has sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into this world to redeem sinful man. Supporting passages under consideration: John 3:16, 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life'; 1 John 4:10, 'In this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins'; Hebrews 1:5, 'For to which of the angels did God ever say, You are my Son, today I have begotten you? Or again, I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son?'

God the Son. Gal 4:4, 'But when the fullness of the time had come, God sent forth His Son, born of a woman, born under the law.' This identifies Jesus as 'God the Son,' the second Person of the Godhead, sent by the Father in the fullness of time and incarnated through human birth under the Mosaic law.

The Lord Jesus Christ. Matthew 1:21, 'And she will bring forth a Son, and you shall call His name JESUS, for He will save His people from their sins.' His given name 'Jesus' (Yeshua, 'Yahweh saves') itself encodes His mission: to save His people from their sins.

He is an eternal person of the Godhead. John 1:1-2, 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God.' Hebrews 1:3, 'who being the brightness of His glory and the express image of His person, and upholding all things by the word of His power, when He had by Himself purged our sins, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high.' The Son is eternally existent, fully divine, distinct in person from the Father yet sharing the one divine essence, and is the active sustainer of all creation.

He was sent by the Father into this world. Gal 4:4, 'But when the fullness of the time had come, God sent forth His Son, born of a woman, born under the law.' John 3:16, 'For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.' The incarnation is the Father's initiative: the Son's coming is a sending and a giving rooted in divine love, with eternal life through faith as the intended outcome.

He was born as the Messiah to the virgin Mary, conceived by the Holy Spirit. Isaiah 7:14, 'Therefore the Lord Himself will give you a sign: Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a Son, and shall call His name Immanuel.' Matthew 1:20, 'But while he thought about these things, behold, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream, saying, Joseph, son of David, do not be afraid to take to you Mary your wife, for that which is conceived in her is of the Holy Spirit.' The Messiah's entrance into the world was both a fulfillment of prophetic sign and a supernatural conception by the Spirit, preserving the Son's identity as Immanuel ('God with us').

He was born without sin and lived a completely sinless life. 1 John 3:5, 'And you know that He was manifested to take away our sins, and in Him there is no sin.' 2 Corinthians 5:21, 'For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.' 1 Peter 2:22, 'Who committed no sin, nor was deceit found in His mouth.' The Son's sinlessness is asserted absolutely: no inherent sin in His being, no committed sin in His acts, and no deceit in His speech. This sinlessness is the indispensable qualification for substitutionary atonement and for the imputation of God's righteousness to believers.

He is both truly Man and truly God (the hypostatic union). Philippians 2:6-8, 'who, being in the form of God, did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made Himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bondservant, and coming in the likeness of men. And being found in appearance as a man, He humbled Himself and became obedient to the point of death, even the death of the cross.' Two distinct lines of evidence are presented: (a) FULLY GOD, John 1:1-2 ('the Word was God; He was in the beginning with God') and Phil 2:6 ('being in the form of God... equal with God'); and (b) FULLY MAN, John 1:14 ('the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth') and Luke 2:52 ('Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favor with God and men'). The Son did not relinquish His deity in becoming man; He humbled Himself by taking on the form of a servant in genuine humanity, while remaining fully God.

Jesus willingly suffered and died on a cross as a substitute for mankind, satisfying the penalty for their sin. 2 Corinthians 5:21, 'For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.' Hebrews 1:3, '...when He had by Himself purged our sins, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high.' 1 Peter 2:23-24, 'who, when He was reviled, did not revile in return; when He suffered, He did not threaten, but committed Himself to Him who judges righteously; who Himself bore our sins in His own body on the tree, that we, having died to sins, might live for righteousness, by whose stripes you were healed.' 1 John 2:1-2, 'we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. And He Himself is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world.' Hebrews 9:28, 'so Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many. To those who eagerly wait for Him He will appear a second time, apart from sin, for salvation.' The atonement is voluntary, vicarious, sin-bearing, propitiatory (turning aside divine wrath), and definitive, accomplished by Christ Himself once for all.

He was buried, rose from the dead on the third day, and ascended into heaven. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4, 'For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, and that He was buried, and that He rose again the third day according to the Scriptures.' Phil 2:8, 'And being found in appearance as a man, He humbled Himself and became obedient to the point of death, even the death of the cross.' John 10:17-18, 'Therefore My Father loves Me, because I lay down My life that I may take it again. No one takes it from Me, but I lay it down of Myself. I have power to lay it down, and I have power to take it again. This command I have received from My Father.' Acts 1:9-11, 'Now when He had spoken these things, while they watched, He was taken up, and a cloud received Him out of their sight... two men stood by them in white apparel, who also said, Men of Galilee, why do you stand gazing up into heaven? This same Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will so come in like manner as you saw Him go into heaven.' The death, burial, resurrection on the third day, and visible bodily ascension are presented as historical events fulfilling Scripture and grounded in Christ's own divine authority over His life.

He is seated at the right hand of God the Father as our High Priest and Advocate. As High Priest, Hebrews 4:15 ('For we do not have a High Priest who cannot sympathize with our weaknesses, but was in all points tempted as we are, yet without sin') and Hebrews 1:3 ('upholding all things by the word of His power, when He had by Himself purged our sins, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high'). As Advocate, 1 Timothy 2:5 ('For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus'); Romans 8:34 ('Who is he who condemns? It is Christ who died, and furthermore is also risen, who is even at the right hand of God, who also makes intercession for us'); 1 John 2:1-2 ('we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. And He Himself is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world'). Christ's present heavenly ministry combines sympathetic high-priestly identification with His tempted-yet-sinless humanity, exclusive mediatorship between God and men as the God-Man, and active intercession that secures believers against all condemnation.

He will return from heaven in the future, visible, in person, fulfilling all prophecies about His life. Acts 1:9-11, 'Now when He had spoken these things, while they watched, He was taken up, and a cloud received Him out of their sight... This same Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will so come in like manner as you saw Him go into heaven.' Hebrews 9:28, 'so Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many. To those who eagerly wait for Him He will appear a second time, apart from sin, for salvation.' 1 Thessalonians 4:16, 'For the Lord Himself will descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of an archangel, and with the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first.' The return of Christ is bodily, visible, personal ('this same Jesus'), and parallels in form the manner of His ascension. It will appear unto salvation for those who eagerly wait for Him, and will involve the resurrection of the dead in Christ.

The Holy Spirit is a Person, possessing the three classical marks of personhood: Intellect (1 Cor 2:11), Emotion (He can be grieved, Eph 4:30; He can be quenched, 1 Thess 5:19), and Will (1 Cor 12:11).

The Holy Spirit is God and is co-equal with the Father and the Son. His deity is shown in Acts 5:3-4 (lying to the Holy Spirit is lying to God). His co-equality is shown in the Trinitarian formula of Matthew 28:19.

Nature of the Holy Spirit: He cannot be seen. He dwelt with the Godhead from creation (Gen 1:2, the Spirit hovered over the waters). He energizes the work of God. In the Old Testament He 'came upon' men for specific empowerment (Judg 6:34, Gideon; Judg 14:6, Samson; Ps 51:11, David's plea that He not be taken away). He dwells / abides in the children of God: He had His abode in Jesus (John 1:32) and He dwells in believers (Rom 8:9), beginning at salvation (Acts 2:38; Acts 10:44). He is sovereign in His coming and going (John 7:37; John 3:8; Acts 2:2, wind imagery, oil imagery).

Cognition / Intellect of the Spirit (eight evidences): 1) He counsels (Isa 11:2); 2) He imparts wisdom (Isa 11:2); 3) He inspired Scripture (Acts 1:16; Heb 3:7; 10:15; 1 Pet 1:11; 2 Pet 1:21); 4) He intercedes (Rom 8:26); 5) He knows (Isa 11:2); 6) He possesses a mind (Rom 8:27; 1 Cor 2:10-13); 7) He reminds (John 14:26); 8) He provides truth (John 14:17, 26; 15:26; 16:13; 1 John 4:6).

Affection / Emotion of the Spirit (four evidences): 1) He experiences joy (1 Thess 1:6); 2) He can be insulted (Heb 10:29); 3) He grieves over sin (Isa 63:10; Eph 4:30); 4) He loves (Rom 5:5; 15:30; Gal 5:22).

Table 5.1, Holy Spirit Word Pictures (eight biblical images and their meanings): Clothing = Empowerment / enablement by the Holy Spirit; Dove = Righteousness of the Holy Spirit; Fire = Visible presence of the Holy Spirit; Oil = Anointing with the Holy Spirit; Pledge = Guarantee with the Holy Spirit; Seal = Ownership of and security with the Holy Spirit; Water = Salvation, enablement, and induction by the Holy Spirit; Wind = Salvation and invisible empowerment by the Holy Spirit. (Cited from MacArthur, John; Mayhue, Richard. Biblical Doctrine: A Systematic Summary of Bible Truth, p. 540, Crossway, Kindle Edition.)

Ministries of the Holy Spirit (overview list): 1) Convicting the world of sin (John 16:8); 2) Regenerating the believer (John 3:5-7); 3) Baptizing into one body (1 Cor 12:13; Acts 2:1-2); 4) Dwelling in the believer (Rom 8:9; 1 Cor 3:16); 5) Sanctifying the believer (Rom 8:13); 6) Sealing the believer (Eph 1:13; Eph 2 / 3:17-18); 7) Guarantee for the believer (Eph 1:14); 8) Comfort for the believer (John 14:16-17); 9) Praying for the believer (Rom 8:26); 10) Giving gifts to the believer (Heb 2:4). [A ministry of 'glorifying the Lord Jesus' (John 16:13-14) appears struck through in the source notes, likely renumbered or consolidated rather than removed doctrinally.]
Ministry 1, Convicting the world of sin: 'And when he comes, he will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment' (John 16:8).

Ministry 2, Regenerating the believer: Regeneration is the work of the Holy Spirit in creating new life in the sinful person, by which act he repents and comes to believe in Christ. Scripture pictures regeneration with four images: spiritual birth, spiritual cleansing, spiritual creation, and spiritual resurrection. Key texts: John 3:5, 'unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God'; John 3:6, 'that which is born of the Spirit is spirit'; John 3:7, 'You must be born again'; Titus 3:5, 'he saved us... by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit'.

Ministry 3, Baptizing into one body: '1 Cor 12:13, For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body, Jews or Greeks, slaves or free, and all were made to drink of one Spirit.' This is the act of adding people to the body of Christ. Its first occurrence was Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4, the disciples gathered, the rushing wind, divided tongues as of fire, all filled with the Spirit and speaking in other tongues). Characteristics of Spirit baptism: (i) It is operative only in this dispensation; (ii) It is experienced by all believers in this dispensation, '1 Cor 12:13: all have been baptised'; (iii) It occurs at salvation and is not repeated thereafter.

Ministry 4, Dwelling in the believer: 'Rom 8:9, You, however, are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if in fact the Spirit of God dwells in you. Anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.' '1 Cor 3:16, Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?' Possessing the indwelling Spirit is therefore the mark of belonging to Christ.

Filling of the believer: 'Eph 5:18, And do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit.' Spirit filling is both God's sovereign empowering for special activity and the Spirit's filling believers with His own character. Two facets of Spirit filling: (i) A sovereign act of God whereby He possesses someone for special activity, this highlights the event of being filled rather than the resultant state of fullness. It occurs in Luke 1:15 (John the Baptist), Luke 1:41 (Elizabeth), Luke 1:67 (Zacharias), Acts 2:4 (the group on the Day of Pentecost). The repetition was caused by a new need for special service, not intervening sin (Acts 2:4; 4:8; 4:31). God did this as His sovereign act without imposing conditions on those to be filled. (ii) The extensive influence and control of the Spirit in a believer's life, an abiding state of fullness rather than a specific event. It occurs in Luke 4:1 (Christ), Acts 6:3, 5 (the first helpers of the apostles), Acts 7:55 (Stephen), Acts 11:24 (Barnabas), Acts 13:52 (the disciples), and Eph 5:18 (believers). This second facet seems to be a close synonym to spirituality. It seems to be something every believer can experience (Acts 13:52, 'the disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit') but not something every believer does experience (Acts 6:3, 'pick out from among you seven men of good repute, full of the Spirit and of wisdom').

Ministry 5, Sanctifying the believer: 'Rom 8:13, For if you live according to the flesh you will die, but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.' Living by the Spirit, listening to Him, and allowing Him to work in our lives helps us in our sanctification process.

Ministry 6, Sealing of the believer: 'Eph 1:13, In him you also, when you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation, and believed in him, were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit.' '2 Cor 1:22, and who has also put his seal on us and given us his Spirit in our hearts as a guarantee.' '2 Cor 3:17, Now the Lord is the Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom.' '2 Cor 3:18, And we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another. For this comes from the Lord who is the Spirit.' Sealing is a reference to God's work of marking the believer with the Holy Spirit (cited from MacArthur, John; Mayhue, Richard. Biblical Doctrine: A Systematic Summary of Bible Truth, p. 1539). Characteristics: (i) Like indwelling, sealing belongs to believers only and to all believers, in 2 Cor 1:22 Paul makes no exceptions, writing to a group in which exceptions could easily be justified; if not all were sealed, how could Paul make sealing the basis for the exhortation not to grieve the Spirit in Eph 4:30? (Citing Ryrie, Charles Caldwell, Basic Theology.) (ii) Sealing takes place at the time of conversion, the 'and' in 2 Cor 1:22 links sealing with the gift of the Spirit as the earnest, and the Spirit is given when we believe (Acts 2:38). (iii) Sealing is to the day of redemption (Eph 4:30, 'do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption').

Ministry 7, Guarantee of the believer: 'Eph 1:14, who is the guarantee of our inheritance until we acquire possession of it, to the praise of his glory.' The Spirit Himself is the down-payment / earnest guaranteeing the believer's future inheritance.

Ministry 8, Comfort for the believer: 'John 14:16, And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper, to be with you forever.' 'John 14:17, even the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot receive, because it neither sees him nor knows him. You know him, for he dwells with you and will be in you.' The Spirit is the promised Paraclete / Helper / Comforter who will be with believers forever.

Ministry 9, Prays for the believer: 'Rom 8:26, Likewise the Spirit helps us in our weakness. For we do not know what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words.' Ministry 10, Giving gifts to the believer: 'Heb 2:4, while God also bore witness by signs and wonders and various miracles and by gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will.'

Statement of Faith, God the Holy Spirit: 'We believe in the Person of God the Holy Spirit, co-equal with God the Father and God the Son. He glorifies the Lord Jesus and implements Christ's work of salvation. He works in this world to convict man of sin, regenerate believers unto salvation, sanctify believers for Christ-like living, and preserve them through eternity. Through the indwelling and filling of the Spirit believers are empowered for godly living and service. The Holy Spirit gives spiritual gifts for the purpose of building up the church.' Verses: Matt 28:19, John 3:5-7, Acts 1:8, John 14:16-17, John 15:26, Eph 1:13, John 16:13-14, 1 Cor 12:4-13, Rom 8:26, 2 Cor 3:18, Gal 5:22-23, Heb 10:15-16, Heb 2:4.

Statement of Faith section on Man (Anthropology). Opens with the credal preamble 'We believe that' which governs every assertion that follows. The document is structured as three doctrinal movements: God's Initial Design, the Impact of Sin, and the Impact of the Cross, each a credal proposition followed by supporting Scripture, and finally a consolidated prose paragraph restating the whole confession.

GOD'S INITIAL DESIGN. We believe that God created man in His own image, without sin, as male and female. God created man to glorify Himself, to be the stewards of this Earth, and to enjoy perfect fellowship, both with God and with fellow man. Supporting Scripture: 'So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them' (Gen 1:27). 'And God blessed them. And God said to them, Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it, and have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing that moves on the earth' (Gen 1:28). 'And God said, Behold, I have given you every plant yielding seed that is on the face of all the earth, and every tree with seed in its fruit. You shall have them for food' (Gen 1:29). The eschatological consummation of fellowship is anticipated: 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God. He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more...' (Rev 21:3-4). And the abiding human telos: 'So, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God' (1 Cor 10:31).

IMPACT OF SIN. As a result of the fall of mankind, all humans are sinners, by their nature and by their choice, and are spiritually dead. The result of this is separation from God, both now and for all eternity. Supporting Scripture: 'And you, who once were alienated and hostile in mind, doing evil deeds' (Col 1:21). 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21). 'And you were dead in the trespasses and sins' (Eph 2:1). 'Therefore, just as sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men because all sinned' (Rom 5:12). 'Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me' (Ps 51:5).

IMPACT OF THE CROSS. The only exception to universal human sinfulness is the Lord Jesus Christ, who is completely perfect and without sin. If a person repents of his sin and trusts in Jesus Christ as Savior, he will then be saved and brought into eternal fellowship with God. Those, however, who do not repent and trust in Christ before death will eternally abide in hell, separated from God, and will suffer His just wrath upon them as a result of their sins. Supporting Scripture: 'He committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth' (1 Pet 2:22). 'And through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross' (Col 1:20). 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21). 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life' (John 3:16). 'But because of your hard and impenitent heart you are storing up wrath for yourself on the day of wrath when God's righteous judgment will be revealed' (Rom 2:5). 'And if anyone's name was not found written in the book of life, he was thrown into the lake of fire' (Rev 20:15). 'Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ' (Rom 5:1). 'The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel' (Mark 1:15).

Consolidated Statement of Faith, Man: 'We believe that God created man in His own image, without sin, as male and female. God created man to glorify Himself, to be the stewards of this Earth, and to enjoy perfect fellowship with both God and fellow man. As a result of the fall of mankind all humans are sinners by their nature and choice and are spiritually dead. The result of this is separation from God both now and for all eternity. The only exception being the Lord Jesus Christ who is completely perfect and without sin. We further believe that if man repents of his sin and trusts in Jesus Christ as Savior he will then be saved and brought into eternal fellowship with God. Those, however, who do not repent and trust in Christ before death will eternally abide in hell, separated from God, and will suffer His just wrath upon them as a result of their sins.' Supporting verses listed at close of confession: Gen 1:27; Gen 1:31; Rev 21:3-4; 1 Pet 2:22; Col 1:20-21; 2 Cor 5:21; Eph 2:1; John 3:36; Rom 2:5; Rev 20:15; Rom 5:1.

Section header: Things to discuss. The document opens as a working agenda enumerating topics that the planning team intends to work through for a future church plant. Items cover both organizational and theological matters. Color legend used in the document: yellow indicates ongoing discussion, green indicates discussion finished.

Why we want to initiate the church plant (individual reasons recorded on 25th Aug 22). Multiple individual reasons were collected. Reasons given included: need for a church in the area; locality-based church making it easier to invite people who live there; need for college ministry in the area; scope for counselling in the area; greater need for the work in the local city; a sense of God's leading; a passion or vision for biblical outworking of the church; a sense that this is the ideal context for the contributor's gifting; new believers and evangelism; leading of the Holy Spirit; desire for a church which focuses on the Word of God; opportunity among young couples; teaching of the Word, good discipleship, and sending people out; community.
Topic placeholder: Vision and Mission. Listed as a topic to be developed but no content was recorded under it in this revision of the document.
Topic placeholder: Core values and beliefs. Listed as a topic to be developed; concrete content appears later in the document under 'Core values'.

Expectations and commitment ('social contract' for meetings, recorded 8th Sept 22). Members are asked to commit to involvement in all current activities as far as possible; if assigned a role, to accomplish it or find someone else who can; if absent, to take responsibility to read the notes and share concerns or thoughts; to be open to talk if anyone seems to not commit much; to give grace especially considering life stages. Participation requires taking initiative, ownership, and providing input. Meetings should also be marked by open communication and confidentiality. Meetings are held on a set time basis: if a topic needs more time, push to next meeting or, with consent of all, continue; it should be acceptable for people to leave at the scheduled end time.

Theological essentials topic. Includes: a Statement of Faith ('What we believe'); a 'triage' of what to discuss first / what the absolute minimal beliefs of the church are. Three triage items called out: the role of women in church, free will versus sovereignty, and the question of formal deacons. A resource is noted: 'Masterpiece of the Ages'.

Additional topic placeholders without developed content in this section: Policies for addressing current events and issues; Roles and accountability; Legal (Registration, Name of the church); Membership; Evangelism; Church Activities; Discipleship; Songs and song book (with sub-item: Bible Study Song book).

Sermons topic, sub-questions to discuss: Who preaches? Length of message. Content (gospel-driven versus 'Christianese'). These remain open for resolution at this point in the document; final positions appear later in the Non-Negotiables section under 'Preaching'.
Additional topic placeholders: Small group / cell group / life groups; Eldership (with note 'Need to start training course right away').

Sunday format topic, sub-questions: offering process (should people go up? can people take money out?); dress code; worship (is music worship?); church layout (pulpit/seating/cross); spoken language; breaking of bread (how to do communion, should it be covered or open, flat or unleavened bread, wine, etc).

Additional topic placeholders: Website and presence in the community; Sunday school; Budget for church / support of ministry; Meeting place on Sunday; Church discipline (e.g. not fellowship) and sin issues; Regular communication of who we are and why we do what we do; Full-time workers question; Should we have a focus on a translation?
Things for training. Six training areas identified: communicating; counseling; culture; expository preaching; language; biblical eldership resources.

Non-negotiable: Meeting Format and Acts 2:42. Types of meetings include the Weekly Sunday gathering, structured around the four marks: Breaking of Bread, Teaching, Prayer, Fellowship. The format begins with reflective silence (10 minutes) and a remembrance meeting with one assigned starter.

Sample Sunday format (recorded 14th Nov 22). 9:30, 5 min reflective silence (assigned person starts). 9:35, 5 min assigned person starts the meeting. 9:40, 25 min open time (songs, exhortation, prayer). 10:05, 15 min giving thanks for bread and cup, distribution and offering. 10:20, 10 min announcements. 10:30, 5 min prayer for announced lists and for the sermon. 10:35, 15 min songs. 10:50, 40 min sermon and closing prayer by the preacher. 11:30, 15 min break. 11:45, 5 min buffer. 11:50-12:30, 40 min split track: Kids (Sunday school, done on rotational basis so they can also join the adult gathering at times); Adults (prayer time; youth meeting / family and parenting; men's and women's fellowship; singing time). Other: testimony time. Ends 12:30.
Other weekly activities under the meeting-format non-negotiable: Bible studies on a weekday.

Non-negotiable topic: Role of men, women, and children, flagged 'will revisit after study'. Sub-points to study: head covering; silence; teaching other women and children; practices under the church order; which meetings come under church order; singing participation for women; roles that should be encouraged for women to take up.

Point of contact (22 Jan 22). Plan: families keep in touch with youngsters. A seekers list is maintained, with each seeker assigned to a family for relational follow-up (specific names of seekers and assigned families have been redacted for anonymization). Seekers should be told officially, perhaps at the next Bible Study, that this is not a mentoring arrangement; rather, all of the families are available to them; each seeker is being assigned to a family simply for keeping in touch and being available; it can be a 'home away from home'; they should not be surprised if someone from that family calls or meets them individually. A note suggests revisiting the topic the following week. The arrangement will continue as long as the seekers are present in the area. One person was tasked with creating a document for this.

Non-negotiable: Membership. Reasons for having formal membership include: figuring out where prospective members are saved and how they are in their Christian life; better governance for the elders and better pastoral care; the members agreeing to the statement of faith; supporting church discipline by their willingness to be under the authority of the church; and creating a clear distinction between members, attendees, and visitors. Members enjoy specific privileges. There should be a formal celebrating public statement when someone becomes a member. The team distinguishes between three categories: legal status; church membership (which includes agreeing to the statement of faith and other commitments); and policy.

Non-negotiable: Ornaments and Marriage practices (16/2/23). On ornaments, wearing, exchanging during weddings (rings), and demanding during weddings (transactions / indirect dowry). The church should teach against harmful practices like dowry; it may be good to brief on this during weddings. On marriage practices generally: any practice that is tied to culture or religion the church should take a stand against. The Mangal sutra is raised as an open question, what is the church's stand if a Hindu convert wears one. A practice that is purely cultural and not linked to any previous religion should be carefully considered, including doing some research and asking the family why they want to do it.

Marriage practices (continued). Marriages done under the church should be marriages between believers. If the parties come from other backgrounds, the elders should discern. On dating: dating among unbelievers is not encouraged; dating among believers is not discouraged because many youngsters will go that route, but the church should help young people reason out why they are dating; conviction about dating is also based on the family. On marriage with a non-believer: after counselling, if they still want to marry, the church will not perform the wedding; their membership comes into question, though they may come back as a guest, and the eldership may walk alongside to see if they can be restored. Whatever discipline is given, restoration must be the key. On divorce: the church should not support divorce, must take precautionary measures to avoid it, and is to draft a position. Premarital counseling is mandatory.

Non-negotiable: Finances and Transparency, Full-time worker. The church should avoid having a full-time pastor. As a church it should support those doing full-time work locally. Commendation should include financial support as needed. 1 Tim 5:17 is cited on honoring elders. The church as a whole should take responsibility for ensuring that serving the church does not end up as only one person's duty.

Finances and Transparency, Budget and giving. Give a gift to those who come to minister from outside, considering their financial state. The budget should align to the church vision. Have special offerings as needed. A budget should be presented for each year showing expenses and allocations. For transparency, provide a monthly update to a forum made available to members. Open questions: what is church offering used for (supporting people in need is one suggestion, needs more discussion); tithing or not; teaching about giving; whether the church gives to anyone (even outsiders) who asks or appeals; look into 80G and tax benefits.

Non-negotiable: Preaching. Preaching should be given to those with the gift. Full-time workers can give a roughly ten-minute update. Teaching style is expository and systematic, with topical messages once a month based on topics determined by the elders. Elders use discretion for potential guest speakers as needed. There is an open forum for feedback to preachers. Sermons can be cut short to accommodate a 10-minute update from a full-time worker. Priority is for sermons to be given by elders, with periodic opportunities for other men from within the assembly. Books being preached through are determined by the elders thoughtfully and prayerfully depending on the need of the church. Preaching training is needed, possibly via a 'preachers club' style setup.

Non-negotiable: Sign Gifts. The church believes that the sign gifts have ceased, there can be a one-time occurrence, but in the sense of a continuous practice in the church they have ceased. When the topic is addressed in church, it should not point to people who believe otherwise; people who believe otherwise should use discretion when they explain it to someone from the church. The position should be mentioned in the church practices.

Sign Gifts, revised position after later discussion (3/8/23). The team does not have to mention this as a statement in the practices, the statement of faith, or any online document; it is only for internal understanding. Stated formulation: 'The sign gifts are currently not used in our church as an everyday practice. But God can use these gifts when he wishes for his purposes and the expansion of His kingdom.'
Non-negotiable: Registration. Need to discuss with someone with experience who can explain the process. Decision: the team will start the registration process but also start meeting in parallel.

Non-negotiable: Partnerships with other churches or organizations. Different scenarios in view: preaching from other churches; financial support; resources support; courses or other things done in other churches that could be promoted or announced; whether to send flyers or gospel meetings to other evangelical churches. Things to consider per case: each case is decided case by case; the partner church's beliefs are considered, including how aligned and how different they are, and the same applies to speakers; why they are doing what they are doing (e.g. for a fundraiser); there must be conversation with the people and churches; if the elders are overloaded they could outsource information-gathering; the elders should take partnership seriously, engage and talk about it; the church hopes to partner with other churches and should be intentional about it.

Non-negotiable: Grounds to leave the church and how to proceed biblically. Recognized grounds: irreconcilable theological and church-practice disagreements. If someone leaves over valid theological differences, they should have an opportunity to communicate their reasons to the church at the elders' discretion; either way, the elders should try to communicate so the body is aware. For personal conflicts within the body that escalate to a member not wanting to come to church, the elders should engage. For any felt need of change in church practice, the member should approach and discuss with the elders before leaving.

Non-negotiable: Church discipline. Anchored in Matthew 18, what does it mean to be 'a heathen or tax collector'; and 1 Corinthians 5, 'do not even eat' with such a person. For those who are repentant but whose sin has affected members in the process: take time with the case; confession to the church may be needed; there may be a discipline process; confession is also made to the persons affected by the issue. When an issue surfaces, the elders must act ASAP and not defer to their next meeting (which may be the next month), elders must be proactive. Open question: what are some reasons for discipline. The document includes an embedded image (handwritten notes on church discipline) and external reference links to bible.org's lesson on biblical church discipline (Matt 18:15-17, 1 Cor 5:1-13) and 9marks' church-discipline primer.

Non-negotiable: Calvinism. The points of election and predestination are the points of a saved person. As a majority, none of the team holds to 5-point Calvinism. The team is addressing Election independently and is not currently addressing Calvinism. Final consensus: both the sovereignty of God in election and the free will of man are present in the team's view. A study is needed on three main points, Unconditional Election, Conditional Election, In-Christ Election. A clear view on Romans 9-11 is needed. The terms 'Calvinism' and 'Arminianism' will not be used in the team's documents. The team will not set a perspective 'as a church' until the three points of election are considered.

Non-negotiable topic: Head coverings and women speaking; church-order vs non-church-order meetings, first contributor's notes. According to 1 Timothy 2:8-15, by design certain roles exist between men and women; men are encouraged to take responsibility and leadership based on the example of created order. 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 expresses similar roles within a local church. The contributor holds the view typically known as 'complementarianism', wherein men and women both have leadership roles in different contexts. First takeaway: women are not to teach or have authority over men, so any praying, prophesying, or exercising of spiritual gifts in ministry toward others would have to be done outside the recognized gathering of the church. Head coverings appear to be a symbol of these roles being acknowledged. 1 Corinthians 11 gives further clarity on why a head covering is worn and why men should not cover their heads, but the contributor is uncertain how it practically plays out, it is meant to display authority in the context of prayer and prophecy in the presence of other men, yet women are to remain silent in the recognized church meeting; therefore the contributor is unsure whether head coverings are needed in the church context. It does seem clear that they are to be worn in any context where women pray or prophesy when men are present. Resolution: to make the most sense of the two scenarios, either 'silence' in church refers to judging prophecy or to the act of teaching and not to praying or prophesying, meaning women should be allowed to pray and prophesy in the recognized church context as long as their head is covered; otherwise, if they are simply remaining silent, the contributor does not see how a head covering is justified or expected based on these passages. References cited: complementarianism explainer at thegospelcoalition.org and women-pastors article at gotquestions.org.

Head coverings, second contributor's thoughts. Five reasons offered for the head covering: (1) Nascent trinitarianism / head hierarchy at four levels, God, Christ, Man, Woman. (2) Creation, the order of creation. (3) General revelation, natural; women to have long hair. (4) Angelology, for the sake of the angels, who are also looking at and present at worship. (5) Church practice, practiced by all churches in the area. There are valid reasons for women to keep their heads covered during the gathering of the church, especially for worship; it is a symbol of submission. The contributor thinks this applies for all worship meetings since it represents the reasons listed. Acknowledged tension: Paul talks about praying and prophesying when that is something women shouldn't be doing anyway, so why mention it, the contributor is studying this further but has not landed on an accurate answer. The contributor also wants the church to define the positive roles of women, not just what is not their role. (A linked Google Doc with the contributor's full thoughts is referenced; URL preserved as a resource link.)

Head coverings, third contributor's thoughts. Section A. Man-woman order. 1 Cor 11:8-9 and 1 Cor 11:3 establish that the head of every man is Christ, the head of the woman is the man, and the head of Christ is God. The glory of Christ is symbolically amplified by the double covering of the women. In this setting Paul (verses 8, 9) draws an unavoidable comparison between Christ and His bride: just as it would be uncharacteristic for the bride to teach and lead Christ, it is uncharacteristic for women to teach and lead in the setting of the church. This order is presumed in 1 Cor 14:34-35, women are to keep silence in the churches and ask their husbands at home if they want to learn anything. Verse 35 encourages them not to be silent but to ask, when home order is applicable.

Role of women, third contributor's positive list (B). (1) As a role model to younger women, Titus 2:4-5: teach the young women to love their husbands and children, to be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient. (2) As doer of good works, 1 Tim 2:9-10: women adorn themselves in modest apparel with shamefacedness and sobriety, with good works. (3) As saved through childbearing, 1 Tim 2:15. (4) As keeper of the home, Titus 2:5. (5) As dispenser of private instruction, Aquila and Priscilla, Acts 18:2,18,26; Rom 16:3; 1 Cor 16:19; 2 Tim 4:19. (6) As servants of the Lord. (7) As gospel witnesses, Phil 4:3. (8) As examples of holy living. (9) As sufferers for Christ's sake. (10) As worshippers.

Final consensus on Head covering, Role of a woman, and Church order / home order (28/7/23). HEAD COVERING, women should cover their head in all church-initiated meetings when men are present; for meetings where men are not present, they don't have to. Other meetings conducted for evangelistic purposes, especially outside the church, do not require head covering. ROLE OF WOMEN, women are to remain silent and not be in any leadership position or any teaching/preaching role, especially during the whole gathering of the church. In corporate worship during breaking of bread, or other meetings of the church when men are present, women should not do any leading/teaching role that represents the whole church. Exceptions are meetings like Sunday school or women's meetings where the actual overall responsibility lies with the elders or another man assigned by the elders to lead that ministry. CHURCH ORDER vs HOME ORDER, all meetings of the church will follow the church order that the elders have put in place out of the Scriptures; the exception may be evangelistic meetings. An external example is cited from another fellowship's statement: an assembly cannot function properly without the ministry of women; God has given speaking and serving gifts to women as well as men, but has placed restrictions on the leadership of women in meetings of the local church, this is not arbitrary or a comment on spirituality or giftedness but a scriptural mandate relating to church order; consequently women (qualified by spiritual gift) are able to lead women's Bible study classes, disciple other women, and teach children, and may be involved in evangelism, hospitality, and other ministries that do not require them to exercise authority over men. A manifestation of women's submission to God's designed order is the wearing of a head covering in meetings of the church where both men and women are present (1 Corinthians 11:1-16).

Goals of the church, selected items. Dressing modestly, also for those who are leading. Baptism: will be done as soon as possible; will require a signed affidavit; will encourage believers to be publicly baptized (they should be willing not to be secret believers); decided case by case; should have a class and discussion with the elders before baptism.

Things to do before we start. Find a meeting place, fallback options are starting at one of two homes; an alternative venue option is also under consideration. Plan a two-month schedule. Finish discussions. Conduct baptism. Plan inauguration program. Decide on a name. Produce a song book. Acquire mics and projector. Official launch dates: September 16-17, 2023, 16th: inauguration meeting and baptism (at one of two named venues); 17th: first Sunday meeting.

Non-negotiable: Eschatology (Covenant / Dispensations). Open question: are the promises for Israel only, and if so, will the New Jerusalem be on earth or in heaven? One contributor's main concern: the church has not replaced Israel, all in agreement on this point. CHURCH POSITION: As a church, we will teach a pre-tribulation rapture followed by 7 years of tribulation, after which there will be a literal 1,000-year reign on earth. Stance pattern: teach one side and acknowledge other views, but have a clear stance as a church. Embedded restatement of the cessation non-negotiable also appears in this section: the church believes that the sign gifts have ceased.

Sign gifts, non-negotiable position recorded 27-July-23 (further explanation). The topic of sign gifts (a.k.a. miraculous or charismatic gifts), whether they have ceased, is a theological debate among Christians. 'Sign gifts' refers to certain spiritual gifts in the New Testament, particularly in 1 Corinthians 12:7-11 and 1 Corinthians 14:1-40, including prophecy, speaking in tongues, and the interpretation of tongues. CESSATIONISM: the belief that certain spiritual gifts, including sign gifts, ceased to be active after the apostolic age or the completion of the biblical canon. Their purpose was primarily to authenticate the authority of the apostles and the early church and to establish the foundation of the Christian faith; once the church was established and the New Testament was complete, these gifts were no longer necessary and gradually ceased. CONTINUATIONISM: the belief that all spiritual gifts, including sign gifts, continue to be active in the church throughout history; there is no biblical evidence or theological reason to suggest they have ceased; the Holy Spirit still empowers believers with these gifts for the edification and advancement of the church today. Note: sincere and faithful Christians hold both views; the debate is intramural; while significant, the topic is not a primary doctrine of the Christian faith and believers can find common ground in the essentials.

Continuation on the ceasing of spiritual gifts as per 1 Corinthians 13:8-10 (1st Aug 23). Commentary: Paul addresses different churches; concerning the church at Corinth, we see misuse of spiritual gifts and Paul's confrontation on the misuse and his focus on love (1 Cor 13:8-10). 'When the perfect comes gifts will cease' is read as occurring after the return of the Perfector. Focus on 1 Corinthians 14:1, where Paul tells believers to eagerly desire spiritual gifts. 1 Corinthians 14:39-40, be eager to prophesy, do not forbid speaking in tongues, and worship is to be orderly. Argument: if we adopt a view of cessation or practical cessation, we will not desire the spiritual gifts that Paul tells us to be eager about.

Survey: problems Paul addressed in his letters to the churches. (1) MISUSE OF SPIRITUAL GIFTS, in Corinth and others, Paul addressed the misuse of spiritual gifts, especially tongues, providing guidance for edification rather than confusion (1 Cor 12-14). (2) DOCTRINAL ERRORS, Paul confronted false teachings, heresies, and distortions of the gospel; e.g. Galatians on adding legalistic practices to salvation by grace through faith; Colossians on syncretistic beliefs mixing other religions with Christianity. (3) MORAL AND ETHICAL PROBLEMS, sexual immorality (1 Cor 5), divisions and quarrels (1 Cor 1-3), dishonesty (Eph 4:25-32), lack of love and compassion (Phil 4:2-3). (4) DISUNITY AND DIVISION, encouraged unity and peaceful resolution; in 1 Corinthians, divisions over leader-preferences (Paul, Apollos, Cephas). (5) PERSECUTION AND OPPOSITION, letters strengthening churches under Jewish authorities and broader societal opposition. (6) TEACHING ON CHRISTIAN LIVING, love, humility, forgiveness, generosity, prayer, daily faithful following of Christ. (7) CONCERNS ABOUT THE END TIMES, in Thessalonians, the return of Christ and the resurrection of the dead.

Dating among believers, non-negotiable position (27-July-23). Dating among believers will not be discouraged because many youngsters will take that route, though it is not encouraged either. Standalone topic flagged but not yet developed: stand on alcohol consumption.

To-do list for launch. Prepare the inauguration letter. Send text to elders of other assemblies (one elder of another assembly was already informed); send to other churches; wait 3-4 days and then send to others. Plan the inaugural program. Plan sermon/teaching for the first two months. See if a website can be obtained before launch.
