Identity statement: This is a local church in a specific geographic region (a particular side of a particular city) that strives to follow the Bible in all that we do as we fellowship together with one heart to glorify the Lord.

Etymology of the name, geographic-locator component: The choice to incorporate a regional landmark (a directional/geographic identifier) into the assembly's name follows a long-standing assembly tradition that emphasizes the idea of a local body within a defined geographic area. This pattern is held to be similar to biblical examples of the 'church at...' formula (e.g., the church at Corinth, the church at Ephesus), where local congregations were identified by their location.

Etymology of the name, 'Bible' component: 'Bible' in the assembly's name signifies commitment to the Bible itself as the basis of the church's life and teaching. Supporting passages: 2 Tim 3:16 (all Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for doctrine, reproof, correction, and instruction in righteousness) and 2 Tim 2:14-15 (rightly handling the word of truth). The practical expression is expository preaching and teaching.

Etymology of the name, 'Fellowship' component: Fellowship is defined as an intentional gathering with one heart and one purpose as a community that follows Christ. Supporting passage: Heb 10:24-25, which exhorts believers not to forsake gathering together but to provoke one another to love and good works.

Core values are framed against Acts 2:42, which describes the early church devoting themselves to four marks: the apostles' teaching, fellowship, the breaking of bread, and prayer. These four marks function as a template for the fellowship's own core values.

Core value: Christ Centered. Anchored in 1 Corinthians 1:10-24, a passage addressing factionalism in the Corinthian church and pointing to Christ crucified as the unifying center against partisan loyalties to human teachers. The Lord's Supper is identified as a concrete, ongoing expression of this Christ-centeredness.

Core value: Discipleship. Anchored in Matt 28:17-20, the Great Commission, where the risen Christ commands the making of disciples by going, baptizing, and teaching them to observe all that he commanded. Practical components named: evangelism (the going and proclaiming) and teaching (instruction in the commands of Christ).

Core value: Church Life & Fellowship. Anchored in Romans 12, which lays out the body-life paradigm, believers as members of one body with diverse gifts, exhorted to genuine love, mutual honor, hospitality, and serving according to grace given. Practical expressions enumerated: holistic small groups, loving relationships, worship, developing and empowering leadership, gift-oriented ministry, prayer, mission focus, and good works (social work).

Vision statement: The vision for this fellowship is to reach the targeted geographic area with the Gospel of Christ through the establishment of a strong and healthy Bible-believing fellowship of disciples of Christ who shine as lights that bring glory to God.

Mission statements (five components): (1) To be a community of Bible-believing disciples of Christ who engage with each other and with the local community. (2) To magnify and honor Christ with our lives. (3) To live life together based on the teachings of Scripture. (4) To proclaim the Good News of salvation through faith in Christ Jesus to friends, family, and neighbors as the Lord gives opportunity to do so. (5) To be a blessing to the local community and fellow residents of the targeted geographic area.

Group discussion prompts on identity, vision, and mission: (a) What aspects of our identity, vision, and mission do you feel unsure or unclear about? (b) Which aspects do you feel like you connect with the most? (c) Brainstorm: What are some actions we could take this week that would help us achieve our mission? (d) What are some ways in which you feel like we have already been achieving our mission and vision? (e) What is at least one thing you can take away from today that will help you engage with this fellowship to better reach our vision?

Invitation under 'What's next?': Those who agree with the fellowship's statement of faith, practices, vision, and mission are invited to unite (engage) with the body and become part of what God is doing through this congregation in the area. The first concrete step for those who have not yet decided to become members is to talk to one of the leaders if they are interested in committing to membership.

Members-only self-examination prompts: (a) Have you discovered your spiritual gift so that you can minister to the body of Christ? (b) Have you determined in your heart to prioritize fellowship with other believers? (c) Have you been intentional in how you live each day so that Christ might be magnified through your life each day? (d) Is the Bible the primary authoritative source of direction for how you live?
Operational notes, language: Services will be primarily conducted in English, with translation provided as needed to accommodate other languages.

Operational notes, geographic intent: The fellowship desires to serve the area along a particular regional corridor and, the Lord willing, intends to meet regularly somewhere in one of several specific neighborhoods in the target area, depending on which door the Lord opens. Prayer is requested for this matter.

Closing pastoral statement: Gratitude is expressed for the encouragement and support received from those present, and an open invitation is extended for any questions regarding the church's identity to be raised after the service.

fields.name: text_to_embed
sample_values: The two ordinances are Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper. Ordinances are practices ordered and commanded by the Lord for his people to observe; these two are considered the primary ordinances of the church.

Baptism was commanded by the Lord Jesus (Matt. 28:18-20) and practiced by the early church (Acts 8:36-38). Baptism is taken by a person who has repented and believed on the Lord Jesus Christ's death and resurrection (Acts 2:38-41). Baptism is given by immersion (Acts 8:38) voluntarily in the name of the Father, Son and Holy Spirit (cf. Matt. 28:19). Baptism is a public confession, an outward expression or symbol of what a person believes internally, identifying with Christ's death through immersion (dead to the old self) and with Christ's resurrection (giving new life) (Romans 6:1-4; Gal 3:25-27).

Cited summary (attributed in source to Piper): 'We believe that baptism is an ordinance of the Lord by which those who have repented and come to faith express their union with Christ in his death and resurrection, by being immersed in water in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit. It is a sign of belonging to the new people of God, the true Israel, and an emblem of burial and cleansing, signifying death to the old life of unbelief, and purification from the pollution of sin.'

Why baptism? Because it is officially ordained by Jesus. Christ commanded it in Matthew 28:19-20: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you.' The early church gave it an important place (Acts 2:38, 41; 8:12-13, 36, 38; 9:18; 10:47-48; 16:15, 33; 18:8; 19:5). Baptism publicly declares that one is now a follower of Jesus Christ. When those in Acts were baptized they were communicating to all around them that they had decided to follow Jesus. It is the outward sign of the inward reality that we are now in Christ, identified with his death (the death of our old self) and his resurrection which gives us new life (Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13). It is one of the foundational truths (Heb 6:1-2 alluded to as '6:1-2').

Romans 6:3-4: 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in the newness of life.' Faith is the means by which we are united to Christ and justified, but we show, say, signify, and symbolize that faith with the act of baptism. Faith unites to Christ; baptism symbolizes the union. Analogy: 'With this ring I thee wed'. The ring does not make the marriage; the covenant-making vows do. The ring shows and symbolizes the covenant. So with faith and baptism. Baptism dramatically portrays what happened spiritually when one received Christ: the old self of unbelief, rebellion, and idolatry died, and a new self of faith, submission, and treasuring Christ came into being. That is what is confessed to the world and to heaven in baptism.

Baptism by immersion. Romans 6:3-4: being buried under water and coming up out of the water signifies rising from the grave. The Greek word 'baptism' means dip or immerse, and most scholars agree this is how the early church practiced baptism; sprinkling and pouring emerge only much later in the historical evidence. Acts 8:37-39: Philip and the eunuch 'went down into the water' and 'came up out of the water,' indicating immersion. John 3:23: John was baptizing at Aenon near Salim 'because water was plentiful there'. Plentiful water is unnecessary if one is merely sprinkling; only a jar would be needed. Note: in the household baptisms recorded in the New Testament, the age of the child is not mentioned, so they cannot be straightforwardly used to establish infant baptism.

Baptism is to be administered in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). There is a holy appeal to God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit to be present in this act. There is no salvation without the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. When we call on their name, we depend upon them, honor them, and declare that this act is because of them, by them, and for them.

Baptism is an expression of faith, only for believers. The order is always: believe and then be baptized (Matt. 3:2-6; 28:19; Acts 2:37-38; 16:14-15, 34). Matthew 28:19: 'make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.' Acts 2:38: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.' Acts 16:14-15: Lydia heard Paul, the Lord opened her heart to pay attention, and after she was baptized she urged them to come to her house. See also Acts 19:1-6. Galatians 3:25-27: 'now that faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian, for in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God, through faith. For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.'

Baptism in the local church. Two parties are involved, and a person does not baptize themselves (Acts 8, the Ethiopian eunuch). The church has been given the mandate to baptize. Acts 2:41: 'So those who received his word were baptized, and there were added that day about three thousand souls.' The Great Commission was given to the apostles and by extension implied to the church; the church recognises the believer and his profession of faith in Jesus, and is in some ways responsible for the believer's growth in the Lord. So it makes all the more sense for a person to be baptised under the church. Baptism is where faith goes public (Acts 2:38-41, those who listened agreed to believe and take baptism, expressing publicly what they believed). It is also tied to being added to the body of Christ, taking part of the communion, and involving oneself in the worship time (the remembrance meeting).

Process for baptism in the local church: (a) Once a person becomes a true born-again believer they should seek baptism (Acts 8:12; Acts 16:33; Matthew 28:19). (b) The candidate will meet with the elders, discuss their faith, and attend some classes on baptism. (c) Baptism will be done as soon as possible. (d) If the candidate is not from a Christian background, a signed affidavit will be wanted declaring that they are taking baptism out of their own free will. (e) Believers will be encouraged to be publicly baptized. They should be willing not to remain secret believers, handled case by case.

John 3:5: 'Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.' Some look at this passage and argue that a person must be baptized first to be born again. But other Acts passages show baptism follows repentance and belief in Jesus, so baptismal-regeneration as the meaning of John 3:5 does not fit the wider scriptural data. Three interpretations of 'water' in this verse are surveyed: (a) water baptism, already ruled out as misaligned with the rest of Scripture and not the focus of this passage; (b) water of physical birth, since Nicodemus asks how one can be born again, some take 'water' as amniotic waters of natural birth, but being born again is not physical (the physical nature is already corrupted by sin) but spiritual; (c) water as cleansing, since Nicodemus, a Pharisee versed in the Old Testament, would likely have understood Jesus to be alluding to Ezekiel 36:25-27 and Isaiah 44:3, where God promises cleansing by water from idols and the giving of his Spirit producing a new heart enabling walking with the Lord. On this reading Jesus is teaching that the cleansing and Spirit-given new heart spoken of in Ezekiel are needed for one to be born again. Resources cited: Josh Moody, John 1-12 for You; The Gospel Coalition article 'Born of Water and Spirit'.

Recap on ordinances. An ordinance is something which has been ordered and commanded by the Lord for his people to practice. Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper are the primary ordinances. Some may also consider washing of feet, marriage broadly, and praying for the sick from James 5; but the primary ordinances considered here are baptism and the Lord's Supper. Ordinances have the idea of a symbol and in themselves do not have any (saving) power.

The Lord's Supper (the Breaking of Bread) was instituted by Jesus Christ (Matt. 26:26-29) and followed by the apostles (1 Cor. 11:23-32). It signifies the remembrance of Christ, the proclamation of his death, and the assurance of Christ's second coming (1 Cor. 11:24, 26; Matt. 26:29). It is a meeting to worship the Lord Jesus Christ and to remember the new covenant he instituted through his death. Why? Jesus instituted it (Matt. 26:26-29); the apostles followed it (1 Cor. 11:23-32); and it brings our attention to Christ and his work on the cross every week.

Requirements for partaking. (a) Those who have believed in Jesus: all the disciples partook and they believed in Jesus; Acts 2:42: 'They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer'; Acts 20:7: 'upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them.' (b) Those who have been baptised: there is no explicit verse stating that only baptised believers may take part of the Lord's table, but it is observed that (1) the regeneration experience and baptism went hand in hand (Acts 2:41-42); baptism shows a person becomes one with the body of Christ, internal in regeneration and external at baptism; (2) baptism signifies our identification with Christ in his death, the death of our old man, and his resurrection giving new life (Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13); (3) since baptism is integral to a person's salvation experience and being added to the body, and since communion is for those who believe in Jesus, it is ideal for those who are born again and baptised to take part of the Lord's table.

Self-examination. Every believer should examine themselves before partaking of the emblems, being aware that it is an act of worship and should be taken in a worthy manner (1 Corinthians 11:27-29). If there is unforgiveness, an issue with a fellow believer, or any sin issue, that should be resolved before partaking (Matthew 5:23-24). Unrepentant believers under church discipline were excluded from the Supper (1 Cor. 5:11-13; 2 Thess. 3:6, 11-15).

Frequency. The Lord's Supper is to be observed on the first day of the week. Acts 20:7: 'And upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them, ready to depart on the morrow; and continued his speech until midnight.' The Didache and Justin Martyr's First Apology indicate that weekly administration of the Lord's Supper existed in the early church (cited via The Gospel Coalition article 'The Frequency of Communion Calmly Considered').

Process of the meeting. During the meeting, the men, when led by the Spirit, can spontaneously share a passage of scripture, prayer, or song in line with the purpose of this meeting (1 Corinthians 14:26). The time will conclude with the communion and then an intentional time to offer unto the Lord financially and in other ways of giving of one's life to the Lord.
Visitors and the Table. Those visiting are welcome to participate from the table if they are following the same practice in another Bible-believing church under the conviction of the elders.

Resources cited in the document: (a) Desiring God, 'What Is Baptism and How Important Is It' (desiringgod.org). (b) Bobby Jamieson, 'Understanding Baptism'. (c) Charles Caldwell Ryrie, 'Basic Theology: A Popular Systematic Guide to Understanding Biblical Truth' (Moody Publishers, Kindle Edition, p. 488). (d) The Gospel Coalition, 'Born of Water and Spirit' article. (e) The Gospel Coalition, 'The Frequency of Communion Calmly Considered'. (f) Josh Moody, 'John 1-12 for You'.

Establishing healthy boundaries with the opposite sex in a Christian church involves creating safeguards that honor God, protect marriages, and maintain personal purity. The goal is to set clear physical, emotional, and digital limits.

When we hear the word boundaries, many think restriction. But biblical boundaries are not fences to trap us, they are guardrails to protect what God has entrusted to us. Boundaries define us: they define what is me and what is not me. A boundary shows me where I end and where someone else begins, leading me to a sense of ownership. Knowing what I am to own and take responsibility for gives me freedom (citing Henry Cloud). Boundaries are not something we walk on; they are something we walk within.

Why boundaries matter, first, to protect your heart. Proverbs 4:23 says 'Above all else, guard your heart.' Jeremiah 17:9 says 'The heart is deceitful.' Therefore do not trust your feelings, your intentions, or your 'I won't go that far' mindset.

Boundaries also protect others and protect your testimony. Titus 1:7 (ESV): 'For an overseer, as God's steward, must be above reproach, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to drunkenness, not violent, not fond of sordid gain.' 1 Timothy 4:12 (ESV): 'Let no one despise you for your youth, but set the believers an example in speech, in conduct, in love, in faith, in purity.'
Boundaries protect future and present marriages. Prioritize your spouse: if married, your spouse must be your top priority, and they should never feel competition from a friend.

Boundaries protect your thoughts, how you see people. 1 Timothy 5:1-2: 'Do not rebuke an older man harshly, but exhort him as if he were your father. Treat younger men as brothers, older women as mothers, and younger women as sisters, with absolute purity.' We care for those we are speaking to and do not want anyone to cross boundaries.

Physical boundaries. 1 Corinthians 7:1 (NKJV): 'Now concerning the things of which you wrote to me: It is good for a man not to touch a woman.' (NLT): 'Now regarding the questions you asked in your letter. Yes, it is good to abstain from sexual relations.' The Greek 'hap-tom-ahee' means to fasten oneself to, adhere to, cling to, covering touch, carnal intercourse with a woman or cohabitation, the Levitical practice of having no fellowship with heathen practices (so celibacy and abstinence from certain foods and drinks are recommended), and to assail anyone. Practical applications: avoid unnecessary touch; be wise with proximity; keep it simple, handshake over hug; avoid confusion or mixed signals if you think you are conveying it; you know when you touch with the wrong emotions; be aware of the setting, you represent the church even outside the church setting.
Emotional boundaries. Do not build emotional intimacy with the opposite gender, especially through late-night calls or deep personal sharing.
Digital boundaries. Private chats are a dangerous space. Emotional affairs often begin here. If you must communicate digitally, keep it clear, short, and purposeful.

Spiritual boundaries. Do not become the 'go-to spiritual support' for a woman, neither for counseling nor for advice, and do not step into ongoing personal counseling. Redirect wisely to someone of the same gender or to mature leaders.

Social boundaries, group settings over isolation. Avoid isolation: do not be alone with the opposite sex in rooms, cars, or private spaces. 'If you wouldn't do it publicly, don't do it privately.' Public over private. Bike rides between a man and a woman, if possible, avoid going together. Avoid traveling late back home, especially with girls. Behave in public spaces in an appropriate way during Bible studies and casual meets. Speak with older people in a respectful way. Dress appropriately in host homes, especially keeping in mind those of the opposite sex are present, and for meetings or Bible studies. Curfew: keep in mind especially on Saturday night that we have to get up for Sunday worship and be active for worship, prepare for the next day. Follow the curfew of the host home. We understand these are times we get to talk about life and what is happening. Host homes, good practices: be respectful of where you stay, aware of neighbors, observe bathroom etiquette, clear tables and help out.
If married: your wife comes first; no competition; full transparency.
Motivation check. Ask honestly: Why am I doing this? What do I enjoy about this interaction? Would I do this if others were watching?
Community and accountability. 'You cannot keep boundaries alone.' Be known by other men, share struggles, and invite correction, call out one another.

Boundaries are principles, with the opposite gender, not to walk on but to walk within. Kinds of boundaries with the opposite sex include emotional, physical, spiritual (men are called to be spiritual leaders, especially in their homes), and social.

Boundaries are for me and for other persons, it is both. They are kept out of love for the other person. God calls us to live for and care for one another; thinking about others changes your behavior.

Where do we get our boundaries from? From how we were raised; from books and influences; from times when we crossed a boundary and realized we needed it; and chiefly from the Bible, what would Jesus do? Ephesians 5:1-2, 15-17, follow God, imitate Him, walk in love as Christ, be wise, be careful. Be holy for He is holy. Jeremiah 17:9-10, the heart is deceitful, desperately wicked; you should not just follow your heart because you feel like it. Proverbs 4:20-23, protect our hearts diligently. 1 Corinthians 11:3, God has designed a certain way; there is a reason; there is an order. Older men or women are to teach those of the same gender.

Practical questions and applications: What are some things we should avoid? What is your motivation in all that you do? Practical ways to keep boundaries, never be alone with a girl in a room or a car; complimenting and not telling anything bad about people or teasing, especially toward the opposite gender or to anyone; chatting with people on the phone and building an emotional connect; if you are doing something you would not do in public, you should not do it; talking to girls is powerful for them, so do not grab intimacy; physical touch, be careful with that too; advice giving, divert to another person of the same sex, preferably an older person, biblically grounded; the boundaries you have might be fine for you but think of the boundaries of that group and the boundaries set by your social system.

On leadership, in some cultures, men come to church thinking that they will have to lead or start a church. 1 Timothy 3:1: if you desire to lead in the church, it is an honourable task, a good thing. Think about leading in the church in some way. If you do not yet fit, work towards it.

Boundaries for leaders and non-leaders sometimes seem different, but most boundaries are relational. 1 Peter 2:9, believers are royal, grand, not common; this is not based on our skills, but God has made us this. We do not walk in arrogance but in confidence. God has called me for this and I work towards it. Proverbs 4:20-27, diligence, a showing of dedication. We are called to be deliberate and to keep our heart in the right place. The Bible says so. We are called to guard our heart.

Church governance under the Lord Jesus is structured around three groups: elders, deacons, and believers. The whiteboard summary outlines three teaching headings for elders: (1) Definition (Acts 20:28; 1 Pet 5:1-2; 1 Tim 3:1); (2) Qualifications, drawn from Titus 1:6-9, 1 Tim 3:2-7, 1 Pet 5:1-4, and Acts 20:28, organized into six categories, Desire, Integrity, The Word, Family, Personal, Relational; (3) Responsibility (Heb 13:17).

On Acts 20:28: the church is the Lord's church; because He owns it, He wants order in it. The Holy Spirit is the one who appoints elders, convictions that the Holy Spirit will put in some men. They are not appointed to serve the church but to serve God. Elders act for the church, they oversee its function, are themselves part of the group ('elder among you'), and the responsibility falls in them. Like a manager on a construction site, the people should fear the elder more than God only as an illustration of weight of responsibility (the point being that the responsibility of the elder is weighty and the elder will give account to God). Shepherding means ministering to the needs of the flock. Elders should have spiritual maturity (presbyter).

Persuasion and convincing (training analogy from Siemens): Ethos, the character of the person who is pursuing/persuading; Pathos, the emotional state of the person who is listening; Logos, the logical state. None of these alone actually works, but the best is the ethos of the pursuer, his character and so on. What is being said here is that the Spirit of God wants this church to hear, so character (ethos) is what most enables the hearing.

Qualification of elders: there are 31 qualifications, grouped into 6 categories, Desire (1 Tim 3:1), Integrity, The Word, Family, Personal, Relational. The accompanying chart compares qualifications listed for elders against those required of all Christians, with Scripture references for each (drawn primarily from 1 Tim 3, Titus 1, 1 Pet 5, and Acts 20). The point of the comparison is that most elder qualifications are not exotic, they are the same character traits expected of every believer, though elders must be exemplary in them.

Responsibility of the church (toward elders): Heb 13:17, obedience and submission. Elders are themselves answerable to God. The elders also have to be recognised by the church, it is not enough that elders are simply present among us; the church must actively recognise them in their role.
Plurality of elders: eldership is a group function. The plurality itself keeps the elders in check.

On the term 'pastor': in Eph 5 'pastors' is used in the plural. It is looked at as a collected group of people, there are those who have pastoral care. The implication is that pastoral function is held collectively by a group, not vested in a single 'the pastor' figure.
Open questions raised but not resolved in this session: What does shepherding mean in practical terms? Are elders to be full-time or not? What is the appropriate tenure of an eldership?

Roles in the church, men and women (28th July 24). The teaching on the role of women in the church is anchored on the meaning of 'head'. 'Head' carries several layers of meaning: (1) Authority, leader. Jesus is the head/leader of angels (Col 2; Col 1:15), this is positional. (2) Physical head, the body cannot live without the head. (3) Source of all thoughts / control center, Eph 4:15-16. (4) Representative, the head represents the unit; Jesus is the representative of all humanity in being the first. (5) Relational aspect of headship, Eph 5: the order is wife-husband-Christ; the example is Christ's relationship with the church. There is a distinction between church order and home order, but Christ being head of the church is the pattern, and the church is subject to Christ. This headship has to be visible in our gathering.

1 Cor. 11:2-16 (28th July). Verse 18, 'when you come together as a church': all the instructions in this passage are for when believers come together as a church. Verse 16, there is no other practice we have (i.e., this is the universal practice of the churches). Verse 3, Christ is the head of Man; Man is the head of Woman (some translations render 'Husband' to 'Wife'; the underlying Greek word is the same); God is the head of Christ. To understand what it means for Man to be head of Woman, look at God being head of Christ. In John, Jesus and the Father are one, yet Jesus says 'the Father is greater than me', not in essence but in role. Both are equal; Christ is not inferior to God, but the role differs. Likewise women and men are not unequal in essence; the world has many problems where women are oppressed by men, but that oppression is not what is being taught here, there is no difference in essence, only in role. (Asked: Can Christ and man be the same? Possibly in humanity, since Christ came as one of us, but He is still head of all humanity.) All of this is to show that Christ is the head of the church.

(4th Aug.) Women have so much role in the church, it is not that they have no role. Examples: Titus 2:4, older women teaching younger women. Acts 18:26, Apollos was a good orator but did not understand something fully; here a married couple takes him aside privately to teach him further. Phil 4:3, women who labored in the church for the gospel. The pattern: women have substantial ministry, including teaching, but the teaching of men is done privately/personally (as in Acts 18:26) rather than in the public leading of the assembly.

The role of the woman in the gathering is to show how Christ is the head. 1 Cor 11:3, the head is the representation of who a person is, like a face on a passport. Man not covering (vs. 4, 7): he is the image and glory of God; in creation God created man first, that is the creational order, and the creational order is to be seen in the church. Woman covering her head (vs. 5): the woman is to cover her head when praying or prophesying. The reasons given by the passage are: (vs. 7-9) the woman is the glory of man, the glory order is God-Man-Woman-Hair; her natural hair acts as a covering, and in covering she represents (and so honors) man. (Vs. 10) 'because of the angels', combined with Eph 3:10, the manifold wisdom of God is shown through the church to the principalities and powers; angels are a display-audience seeing everything from the beginning, and they are looking on as the church gathers.

On the relationship of head covering to silence (1 Cor 11 vs. 1 Cor 14). Some say a woman can cover and pray (i.e., she may pray and prophesy in the assembly so long as she is covered). But there are verses that show silence: 1 Cor 14:33-34, woman is to keep silent (1 Tim 4 also says this). Two problems were occurring at Corinth: (1) women were not covering, and (2) women were speaking in the church and leading. In 1 Cor 11 the apostle is focusing more on the covering of the head at this point; the praying-and-prophesying issue is also a problem but he is not yet addressing it much in chapter 11. He addresses it in chapter 14.

1 Cor 14 is one of the earlier epistles written; very few apostolic writings were yet in circulation. Keeping track of the silences commanded in the chapter, there are three: (1) regarding tongues, to be silent (with stated conditions, e.g., if no interpreter); (2) regarding prophets, to be silent (with stated conditions, e.g., when another receives a revelation); (3) regarding women, to be silent. The first two silences were given with conditions; the silence of the women is given without a stated condition. Does this mean complete silence? Not necessarily. But in any case, in the leading of the congregation, it is better for a woman to be silent.
Agreement to become a member begins with agreement to the Statement of Faith. A prospective member must affirm the church's articulated doctrinal position before formal membership is extended.

The distinctive practices a member is expected to affirm include: (a) local church autonomy, (b) the priesthood of all believers, (c) church government by a plurality of elders, (d) believer's baptism by immersion, (e) weekly observance of the Lord's Supper, (f) head covering, and (g) the role of women. These constitute the practiced distinctives of the assembly that are distinct from the Statement of Faith proper.

The membership process consists of: (a) completing a membership form; (b) the elders reviewing it; (c) a personal meeting with the family, at least two elders are present, and if the candidate is single the elders' family should also be present; (d) attending membership classes covering the Statement of Faith, the church's distinctives, the vision and mission of the church, and church life; (e) signing of the membership document; and (f) an official ceremony announcing the new members publicly.
Membership is only for those who are saved and baptized.

Privileges of members include: (a) being defined as a member as distinct from others (attendees and visitors); (b) attendance at and partaking in meetings; (c) pastoral care and spiritual support; (d) leading ministries; and (e) civic-duty support, which covers (i) attestation by church leadership of testimony and character, for example, commendation letters, membership validation for school admissions, and similar civic duties, and (ii) support for life events such as weddings, funerals, and child dedications. Anyone who is not a member of the church will not enjoy these privileges except for attendance and partaking of meetings.

Expectations of members: (a) agreeing to the Statement of Faith and distinctives, and submitting to the elders; (b) submitting to verses related to church life, including one-another passages, the verse on not neglecting the gathering, the use of spiritual gifts, the verse on elders and submission to elders, conflict resolution among members and elders, loving God and loving one another, and discipleship; (c) the church hopes to promote these for all believers and to help those who need to grow in these matters; (d) members are not to teach anything contrary to the Statement of Faith, if questions arise that are unclear, they should be brought to the elders; (e) the church's primary language is English and the secondary language, where needed, is Hindi; any other language used will be translated to English.

Why are we doing this? Reasons given for instituting formal membership: (a) to discern where prospective members are with respect to salvation and the state of their Christian life; (b) to enable better governance by the elders and better pastoral care; (c) to ensure members agree with the Statement of Faith; (d) to make church discipline workable, since members will have agreed to be under the authority of the elders; (e) to maintain a clear distinction between members, attendees, and visitors; (f) for unity and clarity within the body; and (g) to fulfill the civic duties of the church.
There is to be a formal, celebrating, public statement when someone becomes a member.

Differentiation of categories: (a) Legal, the church's legal-entity standing; (b) Church membership, distinguished by agreement to the Statement of Faith and other commitments; (c) Policy, the church's internal policy framework. These three categories are distinct and should not be conflated.
1 Cor 16:1-3 is an example of intentional collecting of money for meeting the needs of other believers.

Acts 24:17-18 suggests that giving of alms (charity) and offerings (sacrifices) were still practiced in the Jewish community of that day. Though this specific reference was most likely in the context of the offering associated with purification practices of the Jews, it still is consistent with the teaching in scripture regarding offering our lives (including our money) as a sacrifice unto the Lord.
Matt 6:1-4 assumes that followers of Christ will give of their resources to good works and charity and prescribes a level of humble secrecy as opposed to visible demonstrations for one's own glory.

Matt 23:23 is a powerful rebuke aimed at the religious leaders, the scribes and the Pharisees, indicating how they have missed the heart of the law and especially how they have neglected justice, mercy and faith but have excelled in 'paying' tithes. In this context Jesus is not rebuking them for tithing but rather for leaving the heart of the law undone.

Heb 10:8-18 represents Christ fulfilling the Old Testament's sacrificial system once for all, through which all believers are accepted before God. No offerings or sacrifices are necessary to earn God's forgiveness or favor. However, in this great offering that Christ has given on our behalf, He serves as an example for us to give of our lives to God and to one another.
Eph 5:2 affirms the idea of following Christ as an example of offering ourselves to God and to one another in love.
2 Cor 8 also teaches that believers are to be generous towards the needs of others within the ability that the Lord has given them. Paul specifically encourages this 'grace' in verse 7.
Acts 2:44-45 is a powerful example of the foundations of the church being established through the common resources and care for each other that the first church had.

1 Cor 9 talks about supporting those who serve for the sake of the Gospel. Paul not only connects back to an Old Testament principle (Deut 25), but also affirms that the church ought to support those who minister, similar to the example of the priests in the Old Testament.

1 Tim 5 is packed with practical instructions for both personal support and provision within individual families as well as within the context of the church. There is a sense in this passage that there is financial support provided by the church for widows or those with genuine need. Additionally, this passage suggests that elders are worthy of 'double honor' wherein again is a reference to the OT principle of giving to support those who minister and honoring them in doing so (Deut 25).
In Luke 21:1-4 Jesus appreciates the widow who, despite her poverty, gave sincerely of all that she had.

Luke 10:7 provides the reference from Paul regarding a laborer being worthy to receive wages. The context in Luke 10 is Christ sending out disciples for work of the Gospel without any provisions but rather in faith to depend on those of 'peace' who provide for them.

Though the context of Heb 7 is the superiority of Christ as our eternal Priest, there is a strong significance to the fact that Melchizedek, the priest of God, received an offering from Abram. Not only is this a powerful connection between the testaments, it also carries a powerful reference to the bread and wine that Melchizedek presents to Abram in Gen 14.

Gen 4 is the context of Cain and Abel bringing offerings to the Lord. This example precedes the Levitical and Judaic sacrificial system and stands as an example that, from the very beginning of time, people brought offerings to the Lord, ideally bringing an acceptable offering to the Lord.

In Gen 14:18-20 we see the narrative of Melchizedek receiving an offering from Abram as priest of the Most High God. In Hebrews 7, a connection is made to Christ's priesthood being in the order or likeness of Melchizedek to prove Christ's superiority over the priests and sacrificial system of the old covenant. This also serves to support the idea that giving offerings to the Lord preceded the time of Moses and is a practice that goes beyond either testament.
Gen 28 presents Jacob encountering the Lord and committing to give a 'tenth' of all that the Lord gives him.

Deuteronomy 12 and 14 describe additional tithes for various purposes beyond sustaining the priests. God intended for the Israelites to support the practices of worship and faith through charity and the feasts.

Deuteronomy 26 provides the heart behind the tithe offered by the Israelites as they entered into the Promised Land and testified of God's goodness and faithfulness by obediently offering the first fruits to the Lord for the priests, strangers, fatherless and widows. The Lord commanded them to enjoy all the blessings of the Promised Land while declaring His providence and manifesting His kindness towards the needy.
Numbers 18 is where the tithes and offerings are instituted for the priests and Levites as their means of support and special inheritance, given that they are dedicated to the Lord's service.
Nehemiah 13 describes how the storehouses and the practice of tithing for the priests and the house of God were restored in obedience to the Lord by Nehemiah.

Lev 27 provides instructions for those who would desire to 'redeem' their tithes and that which was meant to be sanctified for the Lord. This shows the importance of the purpose behind the commandment to tithe as well as the concern the Lord has for the one desiring to redeem the offering. It was never intended to be a burden upon the giver.

In summary, the concept of tithing as prescribed in the Old Testament does not directly apply to the church. The amounts prescribed in the Old Testament and the purpose of the tithes also no longer directly apply to the church. However, there are clear connections made in the New Testament teachings between the heart or principles behind the tithes and offerings of the Old Testament and believers within the church today. Christians are taught to not only give of themselves 100% wholly to the Lord as living sacrifices (Rom 12) but also to look out for each other's needs and the needs of the Gospel. The example of tithes in the Old Testament to support the purposes of God were separate from 'free will offerings', and both principles are affirmed in the New Testament teaching of willing generosity to the work of the Lord and the needs of believers.

Therefore, in light of these examples and principles in Scripture, a sacred time of prayerful giving is practiced as part of the Sunday worship service in which everyone's eyes are closed as the offering bag is passed, and those who have come with a willing desire and ability to give financially to the purposes of God through the local assembly can do so at that time. Those who are not giving financially during this time are encouraged to prayerfully consider ways in which they can offer their time and other resources as fruits of righteousness (2 Cor 9:8-11) to the Lord, in light of all that Christ has offered on our behalf as had just been considered through the preceding time of the breaking of bread.

Recommended further reading: a Gospel Coalition article on whether Paul quotes Moses out of context in 'do not muzzle the ox'; a Gospel Coalition article on Jesus and Melchizedek; and a Gospel Coalition article giving seven reasons Christians are not required to tithe.
It could also make sense to announce and align different collections for different purposes that are connected to the assembly's vision and mission.

We believe that Salvation is a secure gift that cannot be lost which God offers to all humans by His grace. It is received by faith through repentance and personally trusting Jesus' all-sufficient sacrificial death and resurrection through which God forgives our sins. We, therefore, are saved from the penalty of sin, which is the eternal separation and wrath of God. Once saved, a believer lives in an eternally restored relationship with God as a part of His family. We believe that Christians are called to a life of separation unto Christ, one that exhibits obedience and pursues holiness as we seek to be conformed to His image.
